name: The Carothers Equation: When Polymers Cross the Line
era: The Birth of Synthetic Polymers (1907-1938): The Bakelite Revolution
summary: Working at DuPont's Experimental Station from 1928, Wallace Carothers worked out the mathematical theory of step-growth polymerization. The Carothers equation relates the average degree of polymerization (Xn) to the fractional monomer conversion (p): Xn = 1/(1-p). Its central, non-obvious insight is that step-growth polymerization requires extremely high conversion to reach useful molecular weights -- at 95% conversion Xn is only 20, and it takes 99%+ conversion to reach chain lengths of 100 or more. This theoretical framework guided Carothers' own team to two landmark materials: neoprene (the first synthetic rubber, synthesized April 1930) and, after Carothers shifted to polyamide research in 1934, nylon 6,6 (first made February 28, 1935; publicly announced October 27, 1938, after Carothers' death).

variables.meaning: Fractional conversion (extent of reaction) of monomer functional groups into polymer
